subUnits.title: 5.1 Role of Food in Agricultural Production, Factors Affecting Production and Storage
notes: Food Production and Storage:
Food production and storage are critical for food security, nutrition, and economic development in Nigeria. Biology provides the knowledge base for improving these processes.
Role of Food in Agricultural Production:
• Food as the Primary Goal of Agriculture:
- Agriculture exists primarily to produce food for human consumption
- Food provides energy, nutrients, and building blocks for growth and health
- Nigerian context: Staple foods (cassava, yam, maize, rice) form the basis of Nigerian diets
• Food Security Dimensions:
a) Availability: Sufficient food production and supply
b) Access: Economic and physical ability to obtain food
c) Utilization: Proper nutrition and food safety
d) Stability: Consistent access over time
- Nigerian context: Nigeria faces challenges in all four dimensions of food security
• Economic Importance:
- Agriculture employs ~70% of Nigeria's rural population
- Food production contributes significantly to GDP
- Export crops (cocoa, sesame) earn foreign exchange
- Nigerian context: Agricultural development is key to poverty reduction
Factors Affecting Food Production:
1. Environmental Factors:
• Climate: Temperature, rainfall, sunlight affect crop growth
- Nigerian context: Rainfall variability affects crop yields in different agro-ecological zones
• Soil: Fertility, structure, pH, water-holding capacity
- Nigerian context: Soil degradation in some regions limits productivity
• Water: Availability for irrigation and rain-fed agriculture
- Nigerian context: Irrigation development critical for dry-season farming
2. Biological Factors:
• Pest and Disease Pressure:
- Reduce yields and quality
- Require management strategies
- Nigerian context: Cassava mosaic disease, stem borers affect major crops
• Plant Genetics:
- Improved varieties have higher yields, resistance
- Nigerian context: IITA develops improved cassava, cowpea varieties
3. Socioeconomic Factors:
• Access to Inputs: Seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, machinery
- Nigerian context: Input subsidies aim to improve smallholder access
• Knowledge and Skills:
- Farmer education improves productivity
- Nigerian context: Extension services disseminate improved practices
• Market Access:
- Roads, storage, processing affect profitability
- Nigerian context: Poor infrastructure increases post-harvest losses
Ways of Improving Crop Yield:
1. Improved Varieties:
• High-yielding, pest/disease-resistant, drought-tolerant
• Nigerian context: NRCRI releases improved cassava varieties
2. Good Agricultural Practices:
• Proper planting time, spacing, weeding, fertilization
• Nigerian context: Farmer field schools teach improved practices
3. Integrated Pest and Nutrient Management:
• Combine cultural, biological, chemical methods
• Nigerian context: IPM reduces pesticide dependency
4. Irrigation and Water Management:
• Supplement rainfall, enable dry-season farming
• Nigerian context: Fadama projects support small-scale irrigation
5. Mechanization:
• Tractors, planters, harvesters increase efficiency
• Nigerian context: Government promotes mechanization for youth employment
Causes of Food Wastage:
1. Pre-harvest Losses:
• Pest and disease damage in the field
• Weather-related losses (drought, flood)
• Nigerian context: Quelea birds destroy rice and sorghum before harvest
2. Post-harvest Losses:
• Poor harvesting techniques damage crops
• Inadequate storage leads to spoilage, pest infestation
• Nigerian context: Up to 40% of grains lost post-harvest due to poor storage
3. Processing and Transportation Losses:
• Inefficient processing reduces quality and quantity
• Poor roads cause delays and spoilage
• Nigerian context: Bad roads increase post-harvest losses in rural areas
4. Market and Consumer Losses:
• Over-purchasing, poor storage at household level
• Nigerian context: Urban consumers waste food due to inadequate storage
Methods of Preserving and Storing Food:
1. Traditional Methods:
• Drying/Sun-drying:
- Removes moisture, inhibits microbial growth
- Examples: Dried fish, cassava chips, grains
- Nigerian context: Sun-drying is widely used for cassava, fish, grains
• Smoking:
- Preserves fish, meat with smoke and heat
- Nigerian context: Smoked fish is a common preserved protein source
• Fermentation:
- Microbial activity preserves and enhances nutrition
- Examples: Ogiri, ogi, kunun-zaki
- Nigerian context: Fermented foods are important in Nigerian cuisine
• Salting:
- Salt draws out moisture, inhibits microbes
- Examples: Salted fish, meat
- Nigerian context: Salted fish is preserved for long-distance trade
2. Modern Methods:
• Refrigeration/Freezing:
- Low temperatures slow microbial growth
- Examples: Frozen fish, meat, vegetables
- Nigerian context: Cold chains developing but limited in rural areas
• Canning/Bottling:
- Heat treatment kills microbes, sealed containers prevent recontamination
- Examples: Canned tomatoes, fruits
- Nigerian context: Limited local canning industry
• Chemical Preservation:
- Preservatives inhibit microbial growth
- Examples: Sorbates, benzoates in processed foods
- Nigerian context: Used in commercial food processing
• Hermetic Storage:
- Airtight containers prevent pest infestation
- Examples: PICS bags for grain storage
- Nigerian context: PICS bags reduce post-harvest losses in grains
Practical Activities:
1. Demonstrating Food Storage Methods:
• Demonstrate different food storage methods to students e.g. drying, salting
• Compare effectiveness of traditional vs modern methods
• Discuss suitability for different crops and contexts
2. Post-harvest Loss Assessment:
• Visit local markets or storage facilities
• Observe and quantify post-harvest losses
• Identify causes and suggest improvements
3. Preservation Experiment:
• Preserve food samples using different methods
• Monitor quality over time
• Compare shelf life and nutritional quality
Common Misconceptions:
• 'More production always solves hunger': Distribution, access, and utilization are equally important
• 'Traditional storage methods are obsolete': Many traditional methods are effective and appropriate for local contexts
• 'Modern methods are always better': Modern methods may be costly or inappropriate for smallholder farmers
• 'Food waste is only a consumer problem': Losses occur at all stages from farm to fork
Nigerian Context:
• Nigeria produces sufficient food in aggregate but faces distribution and access challenges
• Post-harvest losses significantly reduce food availability and farmer incomes
• Traditional knowledge of food preservation is valuable but can be improved with modern techniques
• Government and NGO programs aim to reduce losses and improve food security
Conclusion:
• Food production and storage are critical for food security and nutrition
• Multiple factors affect production; integrated approaches improve yields
• Post-harvest losses reduce food availability; improved storage methods can minimize losses
• In Nigeria, improving food production and storage supports food security, farmer livelihoods, and economic development

subUnits.youtubeQuery: Food production storage preservation biology SSS1 site:youtube.com

opts: Access to food economically and physically
quiz.explanation: Food security has four dimensions: availability, access, utilization, and stability; export is not a dimension of food security.

quiz.q: A major cause of post-harvest food loss in Nigeria is:
quiz.explanation: Inadequate storage facilities lead to spoilage, pest infestation, and significant post-harvest losses in Nigerian agriculture.

opts: Removing moisture to inhibit microbial growth
quiz.explanation: Sun-drying removes water from food, creating an environment where microbes cannot grow, thus preserving the food.

quiz.q: A traditional Nigerian method of food preservation is:
quiz.explanation: Fermentation is a traditional Nigerian preservation method used for foods like ogi, ogiri, and kunun-zaki.

quiz.q: PICS bags help reduce post-harvest losses by:
opts: Creating an airtight environment that suffocates pests
quiz.explanation: PICS (Purdue Improved Crop Storage) bags are hermetic; they create an oxygen-depleted environment that kills storage pests without chemicals.

quiz.q: A factor affecting food production in Nigeria is:
quiz.explanation: Rainfall variability affects crop growth and yields, especially in rain-fed agricultural systems common in Nigeria.

quiz.q: Improving crop yield can be achieved by:
quiz.explanation: Integrated approaches combining improved varieties, good practices, and pest/nutrient management sustainably increase yields.

quiz.q: Understanding food production and storage is important for Nigeria because it:
opts: Supports food security, reduces losses, and improves farmer incomes
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of production and storage helps address food insecurity, reduce post-harvest losses, and improve livelihoods in Nigeria.

opts: Using heat and smoke to dry and inhibit microbes
quiz.explanation: Smoking uses heat to dry fish and smoke compounds to inhibit microbial growth, preserving the fish for longer storage.

quiz.q: A common misconception about food waste is:
opts: Losses occur only at consumer level
Losses occur at all stages from farm to fork
quiz.explanation: Food losses occur at production, post-harvest, processing, distribution, and consumption stages; addressing all stages is essential.
